preamble: The transformation of the close back rounded vowel /u/ into the voiceless labiodental fricative /f/ is a rare but attested phonetic shift, primarily observed as a form of high vowel fricativization. This change involves the vowel losing its vocalic quality and acquiring a fricative manner of articulation, while retaining a labial feature.
phoneticEffects: Fricativization, Consonantization, Labialization (retention of labial feature), Loss of vocalic quality
sources: Janse, M., & Vandewalle, J. (2019). The history and etymology of Cappadocian fšáx 'child' Pharasiot fšáxi 'boy'. In M. Janse, B. D. Joseph, & A. Ralli (Eds.), Contact Morphology in Modern Greek Dialects (pp. 1-14).
Janse, M., & Vandewalle, J. (2019). High Vowel Fricativization and Chain Shift. ResearchGate.
Janse, M., & Vandewalle, J. (2020). Contact Morphology in Modern Greek Dialects. ResearchGate.
